UX writing Content strategy Information architecture Design systems Expedia Group · 2019

Visibility Booster — unifying two ad tools into one

Combining a cost-per-click and cost-per-acquisition advertising tool into a single, partner-friendly product — from zero to executive sign-off across a design swarm, sprint, and months of prototype refinement.

2→1
Ad tools merged into a single unified flow
5-step
Guided creation flow with inline recommendations
Exec ✓
Signed off after months of sprint iteration

Overview

Two tools, one confusing experience

Expedia Group offered hotel partners two separate advertising products — a cost-per-click (CPC) tool and a cost-per-acquisition (CPA) tool — each with its own flow, language, and mental model. Partners had to navigate both independently, often without understanding the strategic difference between them.

The task: design a single unified “Visibility Booster” product that merged both payment models into one coherent experience, guided partners through setup with smart recommendations, and surfaced performance in a way that was both motivating and actionable.

UX writing Content strategy IA Figma Design swarm Prototype iteration
Marketing hub — entry point where partners see their booster performance and quick tasks

My role

Content designer & IA lead

I was embedded across the full design process — from the initial swarm through sprint refinement to executive presentations. My work spanned the words on screen, the structure of the flow, and the logic of how information was revealed at each step.

UX writing & microcopy

Wrote all interface copy across the creation flow, dashboard, performance views, and confirmation states. Defined plain-language standards for financial terms (base bid, cost per click, daily budget) and created a consistent pattern for recommendation language.

Information architecture

Designed the 5-step creation flow structure (Dates → Strategy & budget → Audience → Payment → Create), advocating for progressive disclosure so partners weren't overwhelmed before they'd committed to a payment model.

Content strategy

Defined the recommendation framework — identifying which decisions partners struggle with most (payment model, bid amount, date selection) and how the product should communicate data-driven guidance without being prescriptive.

Stakeholder alignment

Contributed to design swarm facilitation, documented content rationale in the prototype, and presented copy and IA decisions to executives as part of the sign-off process.

The challenge

Making ad tech feel approachable

Hotel partners are not marketers. Most have never managed a digital ad campaign. Terms like “base bid,” “cost per click,” and “return on investment” carry real financial stakes — but were being presented without sufficient context or guidance.

At the same time, the product needed to give partners enough control to feel confident, while surfacing Expedia's data and recommendations to reduce the decision burden. Too much autonomy and partners would under-bid or skip setup; too much automation and they'd distrust the product.

Core content tension: How do you write for someone who needs to feel in control while also trusting your recommendations — for a financial decision they've never made before?

Inline recommendations for dates, budget, and audience — surfaced at each decision point

Approach — Flow & IA

A guided creation flow with progressive disclosure

One of my core IA contributions was the step-by-step sidebar — a persistent progress tracker that shows partners where they are and, crucially, what they've already decided. Early designs showed only step labels; I advocated for adding confirmed details inline so partners could review their choices without backtracking.

Before (left): steps show labels only. After (right): confirmed details shown inline for at-a-glance review
Strategy & budget step — performance predictions update in real time as partners configure their spend

Approach — UX writing

Recommendations in plain language

Rather than exposing raw data to partners and asking them to interpret it, I wrote recommendation copy that translated Expedia's algorithmic signals into human decisions. For date selection, this meant surfacing holiday demand and traffic forecasts as card-based suggestions partners could accept with one click. For budget, it meant showing market averages and leader benchmarks side-by-side with a recommended value.

Strategy selection cards — each option shows a visual representation, plain-language label, and projected room nights
Animated prototype showing the guided creation flow from dates through to confirmation

The “Help me decide” affordance on the payment model step was one of the highest-impact content decisions: naming the action plainly, using first-person for the partner, and making it feel like asking a knowledgeable colleague rather than reading a help article.

Approach — Confirmation

A summary that builds confidence, not anxiety

The pre-creation summary modal was a late-stage addition driven by partner research showing users felt uncertain right before committing to an ad. I wrote the summary to confirm the three most important decisions in plain terms — billing trigger, schedule, and audience — with a gentle warning state for incomplete funding.

The structure (checkmarks for confirmed details, a warning for the draft state) used visual hierarchy to reassure rather than alarm — a fine line when money is involved.

Confirmation summary — three key decisions restated in plain language before the partner commits

Approach — Performance

From numbers to narrative

Post-launch, partners needed to understand whether their booster was working. The performance dashboard presented a challenge: the same metrics (impressions, page views, room nights, revenue) could be read as good or bad without context. I worked with the design team to ensure every metric was anchored to a “without booster” baseline, and wrote the increase labels to tell a before/after story rather than just showing a number.

Performance dashboard — each metric anchored to a “without booster” baseline for context
Detailed booster performance — bar and line charts compare property page views against competitive set

Approach — Activation

Personalised outreach to drive partner adoption

A key part of the content strategy extended beyond the product interface: partners who hadn't yet activated a booster needed a reason to try. I contributed to the personalised email framework, which used Expedia's property data to surface a specific, pre-configured booster recommendation for each partner — with a headline tied to their unique opportunity (e.g. mobile shopper reach) and a low-friction “try it free” CTA.

Content principle: Make the value specific before you ask for the click. A generic “boost your visibility” email is easy to ignore; a message naming your property, your opportunity, and your potential uplift is not.

Personalised activation email — property-specific headline, pre-configured booster, low-friction CTA

Outcomes

What we achieved

↗
Partner activation
Unified flow and plain-language guidance reduced setup friction. The personalised email campaign drove partner adoption by surfacing pre-configured boosters with property-specific value props.
↓
Reduced decision complexity
Inline recommendations, contextual benchmarks, and a progressive 5-step flow eliminated the need for partners to interpret raw ad data or understand bidding strategy from scratch.
✓
Executive sign-off
The prototype was refined across a full design swarm and sprint process, with content rationale documented throughout. The product received executive approval for development.

Reflection

What I learned

This project reinforced that content design is most powerful when it's embedded earliest — before the layout is fixed and before the data model is locked. The decisions about what to show and when to show it are content decisions, not just design ones.

Working on a financial product taught me to be precise about consequence. Every label, recommendation, and confirmation message had real monetary implications for partners. Plain language wasn't just a style choice — it was a trust mechanism.

And running a design swarm taught me how to defend content decisions under time pressure — to articulate why a word matters, not just that it does.

Elliot Crumpley ← Back to all work
